cataloging_subject.label: La Estorie del Evangelie
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous late-thirteenth-century Middle English metrical life of Christ represented in CME00079 by Gertrude H. Campbell's 1915 transcriptions of two manuscript versions. The record describes one work, not two independent works and not an eclectic reconstruction. Its content parts identify the separately serialized Dulwich and Bodleian witness representations so dates, loci, and lineage remain scoped. The work is distinct from Dulwich College MS 22 and Bodleian MS. Add. C. 38 as complete physical objects, Carleton Brown's rotographs, Campbell's article, the University of Michigan encoding, and the checked-in XML.

catalog_summary.title: La Estorie del Evangelie
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: Late thirteenth century; earliest surviving witness circa 1300; source edition published 1915
catalog_summary.region: Fenland zone of south Lincolnshire, north-west Norfolk, or north-east Cambridgeshire; exact composition place uncertain

identifiers.scheme: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English

titles.supplied_by: Conventional scholarly title from the Anglo-Norman heading preceding the Vernon fragment
notes: The title is conventional for the Middle English work; it is not a collective title invented for the two CME witness streams.

titles.value: I ceste liuere est escrit la estorie del Euangelie en engleis
titles.supplied_by: Anglo-Norman heading in the Vernon Manuscript
notes: The longer heading continues by listing the Annunciation, Nativity, Passion, Resurrection, Ascension, glorification, Second Coming, Judgment, and presentation in body and soul.

titles.value: Nouum testamentum domini nostri ihesu christi
titles.supplied_by: Marginal title in MS. Add. C. 38 reported by Campbell

titles.value: Sum wyle i was wyt sinne ibunde / Ant sinne me hauid cast to grunde
Ant of nazaret him bi-gan to hye / Ant to bethleem weys guye
notes: This is the accidental end of the 519-line Dulwich fragment, not the work's authorial explicit.

titles.value: Sum tyme i was with loue bounde / and synne me hath kest to grounde
that o god is and euer shal be / fadir and son and the holi gost in trinite. Amen
notes: The witness reaches a devotional Amen; modern reconstruction still treats every surviving version as incomplete relative to the wider text tradition.

agents.name: Gertrude H. Campbell
notes: Editor of the 1915 immediate source article and both encoded witness texts.

scope.description: Immediate source edition and witness transcriptions

date_statements.display: Probably composed in the late thirteenth century
notes: The date is reconstructed from the circa-1300 Dulwich witness and textual history; it is not a dated authorial colophon.

date_statements.display: The earliest surviving witness, Dulwich College MS 22, was copied circa 1300
notes: This dates the surviving copy and establishes a terminus ante quem, not an exact composition year.

places.label: Fenland: south Lincolnshire, north-west Norfolk, and north-east Cambridgeshire

scope.description: Reconstructed broad zone, not a certain settlement

notes: Modern scholarship places the original in an East Midlands/Fenland zone from shared linguistic features; D itself is localized to South Lincolnshire and B to Worcestershire. Copy dialects are not transferred mechanically to composition place.

scope.description: Reconstructed dialect of the original behind the seven witnesses

language_statements.label: Latin

scope.description: Recurring manuscript rubrics and scriptural or patristic quotations within the Middle English narrative

notes: Modern English editorial headnotes and footnotes belong to Campbell's edition layer and are not exposed as a work-language facet. The Anglo-Norman wording supplies the conventional title through the Vernon witness but is not part of the two CME text streams.

form_statements.description: A metrical narrative preserved in monorhyming quatrains and rhymed couplets. Recurring Latin rubrics and quotations do not make the work mixed prose and verse.

subject_statements.label: Death, salvation, and the Last Judgment
Parallel manuscript versions and textual transmission

scope.description: Discovery subject of the CME representation and Campbell edition

summaries.value: La Estorie del Evangelie is an anonymous late-thirteenth-century Middle English metrical life of Christ. It combines Gospel narrative from the Annunciation and Nativity through the Passion, Resurrection, Ascension, and Last Judgment with devotional exhortation, bestiary moralization, apocryphal episodes, and recurring Latin rubrics. CME00079 serializes Gertrude H. Campbell's 1915 parallel edition of two substantially different manuscript versions: an early 519-line Dulwich fragment and a longer, heavily revised Bodleian text. Campbell's modern English introduction and concluding source notes are omitted. Modern scholarship recognizes seven mutually non-derivative surviving witnesses; the other five are recorded as collateral transmission evidence rather than sources of the two encoded texts.

The Dulwich version opens with a penitential invocation and moralizations of the hart, serpent, and eagle, then narrates the annunciations and births of John the Baptist and Christ. Its surviving 519 lines end as Joseph and Mary travel from Nazareth to Bethlehem.

The Bodleian version begins with the same penitential frame in a modernized and abridged textual form, continues through Christ's birth, ministry, Passion, descent, Resurrection, Ascension, Pentecost, and the Last Judgment, and closes with a devotional vision of heavenly bliss and a Trinitarian Amen.

extent.description: Two serialized witness-version divisions contain 2,222 XML L elements in 133 LG groups: 519 Dulwich lines and 1,703 Bodleian line elements. Campbell's shared lineation reaches D 519 and B 1879, while Millward's reconstructed tradition places the same B version from the beginning through reconstructed line 2435. The encoding includes 98 PB elements over the overlapping physical printed range pp. 545–610, 433 MILESTONE elements, 86 notes, and 1,837 italic spans. These are encoding metrics, not an authorial line count or a count of distinct print pages.

content_parts.label: Dulwich College MS 22 version
content_parts.summary: Campbell's transcription of the 519-line opening fragment, serialized from the left-hand parallel print stream.

content_parts.label: Bodleian MS. Add. C. 38 version
content_parts.summary: Campbell's longer Bodleian text, restored from two-verses-per-manuscript-line to couplet form and serialized after the Dulwich stream.

related_works.title: The canonical Gospels
notes: Ultimate narrative source. Campbell argues that the author probably used a Gospel harmony, breviary, or other compilation rather than working directly and continuously from the Vulgate.

related_works.title: Historia scholastica
notes: Peter Comestor's Latin work is a probable important intermediary for some biblical and apocryphal material, but neither Campbell nor current scholarship identifies one complete direct source or the author's physical exemplar.

evidence.locator: IDG; TEXT/@LANG; BODY/DIV1/DIV2[1–2]; HEADNOTE, HEAD, L, LG, P, PB, MILESTONE, NOTE, HI, ABBR, and TRAILER
evidence.summary: Direct evidence for identifier, two witness representations, titles, incipits and endings, Middle English and embedded Latin, verse form, contents and subjects, line structure, print and manuscript locators, and editorial representation.

evidence.locator: whole XML tree and DIV2-scoped XPath counts
evidence.summary: Verifies two DIV2 streams, 2,222 L elements, 133 LG groups, stream-specific line/group/page/milestone/note counts, 98 total page breaks over overlapping printed pages, and 1,837 italic spans.

evidence.citation: Gertrude H. Campbell, 'The Middle English Evangelie,' PMLA 30.3 (1915), 529–613
evidence.locator: article pp. 529–613; encoded section VI pp. 545–610
evidence.summary: Primary source for preferred and alternate titles, anonymous authorship, manuscript and edition layers, late-thirteenth-century terminus, dialect and source arguments, text contents, form, incipits/explicits, lineation, editorial practice, and the exact selection boundary.

evidence.citation: Sara M. Pons-Sanz, 'Norse-derived vocabulary in La estorie del evangelie,' Folia Linguistica Historica 42.2 (2021), 461–491
evidence.summary: Current peer-reviewed evidence for the late-thirteenth-century date, broad Fenland origin, seven-witness census, individual manuscript loci, line counts, textual relations, form, and probable Gospel/Historia scholastica source context.

evidence.citation: George F. Warner, Catalogue of the Manuscripts and Muniments of Alleyn's College of God's Gift at Dulwich, vol. 1 (1881), 344–346
evidence.summary: Primary catalog evidence for Dulwich shelfmark, support, total extent, composite context, inserted quire, early witness date, fragmentary state, incipit, and adjacent contents.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, MS. Add. C. 38, manuscript_153
evidence.summary: Official evidence for exact shelfmark, identifiers, manuscript title, Middle English, early-fifteenth-century date, English origin, parchment support, and South English Legendary context.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Vernon Manuscript complete Digital Bodleian IIIF object
evidence.locator: La Estorie del Evangelie, fols. 105r–105v, canvases 193–194
evidence.summary: Official and visual evidence for the conventional title's witness context, fragment locus and extent, manuscript date and region, and complete public facsimile.

evidence.citation: Carl Horstmann, ed., The Minor Poems of the Vernon MS, part 1, EETS OS 98 (1892), 1–11
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for the prior Vernon edition, Anglo-Norman heading, conventional title, opening text, and source Campbell consulted.

open_questions.question: What is the current authoritative Dulwich folio range for the fragment?
next_steps: Ask Dulwich College Archives to confirm whether fols. 81v–85v is the current range and explain Campbell's shorter introductory locator.
Compare a complete surrogate against the encoded folio milestones.
Keep the current mapping provisional until confirmed.

open_questions.question: How should Campbell's 'complete text' description for B be reconciled with the modern reconstruction of seven individually incomplete versions?
next_steps: Separate a coherent witness ending from completeness relative to the lost archetype.
Retain both attributed claims rather than flattening them into one extent statement.

open_questions.question: What compilation mediated the work's Gospel, Peter Comestor, bestiary, apocryphal, and homiletic materials?
next_steps: Review Millward's source analysis and later biblical-literature studies.
Keep Peter Comestor probable and scoped rather than naming an unsupported physical exemplar.
Distinguish sources for local passages from the main narrative source.

open_questions.question: Can the original poem be localized more narrowly than the broad Fenland zone?
next_steps: Preserve the distinct copy dialects of D and B.
Do not convert reconstructed dialect geography into a certain composition place.

notes: The two content parts are witness representations needed for scoped lineage and metrics; they are not authorial chapters or separate works.
Campbell's line numbers and the XML L count differ from Millward's reconstructed lineation because each witness omits, reorders, or revises material. All counts are labeled by layer.
The complete cached Vernon facsimile supports title and transmission research but is not the physical source of either continuous CME text.
Public or cached access never by itself authorizes redistribution. The Vernon images are CC BY-NC 4.0; any future Dulwich or Add. C. 38 delivery needs item-specific terms.
